pages.transcription:
Confidential

Dear Sir,

A conference has taken place
this day, with Sir William Rule at the
Admiralty; respecting your journey to
Plymouth. You will with this receive
a public letter on the subject. As your
report may be a document of some
importance to your own interests as
well as the public service, let me hint
to you the propriety of making it -
singly, without the interference of
M^r Lukin - On your return I shall be
glad

glad to see you, and if there should
be any circumstances respecting the
construction of Ships or the usage of
the service that you wish to be inform'd
of before you transmit the report, I
shall be glad to afford the information.

I am with respect
Dear Sir
Y^r devoted S^t
J Knowles

Navy Office
17^th July 1812.

I presume that you will stop at Blandford:
as that place is within two miles of Spetsbury
where M^r Rackett lives, perhaps you may
see him -

[Address panel of a folded letter, with a red wax seal]

Private

M^r Sowerby
Mead Place
Near the Asylum
Lambeth

[Written vertically in bottom left corner:]
Knowles

Private

Navy Office 18^th July 1812.

Dear Sir,

I need not repeat to you how much alive I am to the object of your visit to Plymouth; and I trust that you will consider the hints that I am about to give you, as proceeding from an ardent wish that you may have all possible information on the subject, and that no circumstance, however minute, may escape your observation, while you are on the spot.

The fact that you mentioned to Sir William Rule, of timber that has been heated being more subject to dry rot than other wood, made me enquire into the case; and I find that it is proved by practice. The King's Yacht has a galley between decks, and the parts where it stands were subject to a great degree of heat while attending His Majesty at Weymouth: Upon the planks being removed, some time after her return, they, as well as the frame which was exposed to this heat, were found in a complete

state

state of decay from the growth of Fungi, while the other parts of the Vessel were free from vegetation.

It has been the object of Mr Lukin to destroy the Boletus by drying up the moisture by intense heat: for this purpose, Stoves were placed in the Hold and upon the several decks - The heat in the Hold must have been very great, as immersion would prevent the escape of the air; while that on the decks, I imagine, was not at a very high degree of temperature. Is it to the great heat under water that you attribute the absence of the plant, or to the action of salt water? It may be right to mention that the middle and upper decks were particularly shrunk by heat and of course admitted Athmospheric air. Can the state of the Thermometer on the several decks be ascertained?

It is not only in this Ship that Pitch-pine has proved more subject to dryrot than other wood, but also in the Bellepoule Frigate. I saw some specimens taken from her, which were in the same state as that which you saw at this Office. In fact, from all the observations and enquiries that I have been enabled

to make

---

to make, timber grown in America is very subject to decomposition from that cause.

I should be sorry to misrepresent any circumstance or take up only one side of the question - Many Ships that have, to the best of my knowledge, been constructed of English oak only, have also been destroyed by Boletus lachrymans. On this subject you can gain some local information: the Foudroyant of 80 Guns built at Plymouth in 1798 was nearly destroyed by it after three years' service - the Ajax, 74, built by contract in 1798, was also found in the same state after the like service - and many other instances too numerous to detail here.

I beg to direct your attention to the small chocks through which bolts pass to connect the timbers - they strike me not only to be injurious to the circulation of air between the frames, but also, by confining the carbonic acid gas which issues from the timber (more particularly where sap is left) to promote the growth of vegetation. These Chocks are to my mind, not only useless in themselves, but injurious.

It will be well to consider whether the Ship

is

is sufficiently stripped to ensure her being free from the Fungi; for a recurrence of the evil, after her repair, would be extremely unfortunate.

Some of the Materials (Planks) that have been taken of are intended to be replaced: it will be right to ascertain whether they are not so far infected as to endanger her future safety.

With every good wish for your pleasant journey, I remain

Dear Sir,

Yours very truly

John Knowles

Private

Dear Sir

We have this day received an
order from the Admiralty Board
directing that the repairs of the -
Queen Charlotte shall be proceeded
with according to your suggestions
without further reference to
M^r Lukin's reports. And conveying
their Lordships flattering marks
of attention [inserted above: in words] to the
fullest

fullest extent and directing the -
Navy Board to acq^t you of their
Lordships approbation - so that
you will receive a public letter of
thanks; I thought you would
like to have full & early information
on this point - I am D^r Sir

Very truly Y^rs
Jno Knowles

Navy Office
24 Aug^t 1812

James Sowerby Esq^r
Near the Asylum
Lambeth

Dear Sir

I am ordered to Plymouth.
tomorrow night to meet Sir W^m Rule
there, to examine the Queen Charlotte
in those parts on which planks.
Where, when you saw the ship.

Have you any commands.
there, or do you wish to have
any further information respecting
that ship? I am D^r Sir

y^r devoted &c
J Knowles

Navy Office
3 Sept^r 1812

[Address panel, written upside down:]

James Sowerby Esq^r
Mead Place
Lambeth

[Docket, written sideways:]

J. Knowles
3 Sept 1812

My dear Sir

Let me return to the convers
ation that we had when you
were last here - I advise you
to state to the Board how many
days you were at Plymouth
inspecting the Queen Charlotte
that you attended the lettings to
Deptford & Woolwich Yards by
[unclear: already?] three Reports on the dry
their desire, that you have [inserted above: not frequent of late]
superintended the making of
the Hygrometers, and had
communication with the Men on
the

[Top letter:]

the subject and argueth some remuneration for your services. This will bring the subject before them & ensure you a reward - I advise you to do this and it is M^r Seppings's advice also that it should now be done

The Vigo a ship built about three years since and heated according to Lukin's plan is in a bad state from the dry rot, the fungus is seen upon the beams, decks &c exteriorly on the timber in the inside of the ship, do you think if we were to fumigate the ship with brimstone & Nitre it would stop the vegetation, this is a notion of M^r Seppings, & he wishes to hear your opinion - Excuse this hasty scrawl

Yours always
JKnowles

Navy Office
3^d Dec^r 1813

---

[Bottom letter, draft reply:]

My Dear Sir

I recieved yours late last night. I would by no means venture to fumigate the Vigo at all, as I concieve it will make the case still more desperate. I venture thus much to M^r Seppings and you knowing your whole desire is to do good. I shall remember both his and your instructions and am, with kind respects to M. S. yours very faithfully
Ja^s Sowerby

To J. Knowles Esq^r

[Address panel of a folded letter]

[Top left, in ink:]
[unclear: M^r Seppings Sely?]

[Postmark, red ink, circular:]
3.DEC
1813

[Postmark, red ink, shield shape:]
T.P
P^y Paid
Strand Co

[Address:]
James Sowerby Esq^r
Mead place
near the Asylum
Lambeth

[Bottom left, in pencil:]
M/1000

[Top center, in pencil:]
Noted

My Dear Sir

I beg to trouble you with this to say that I have assayéd to follow M^r Seppings & your own kind advice regarding my services to the Navy; for which accept my most grateful thanks. I can, at present however, only say, that if I am to reckon my future services on the basis of what I have done (the common rate of salvage for my advice on the Queen Charlotte and something on account of the example by which so many valuable ships may now be saved, might be considered a reasonable indemnification for the loss of time and the trouble I have had for nearly two years past) [inserted above: I believe] upon this part of my reports only, the Navy Board would be indemnified in paying me some compliment if only as surety for future consideration: While this is neglected or forgotten I really think important sources may be lost, and this neglect gives me no encouragement for future exertions, which, by the bye, I grieve to see necessary, while it must be irksome to a generous mind

to proceed upon such unpromising grounds
I am

    Dear Sir

        Yours very truly
        Ja^s Sowerby

To John Knowles Esq^r
Navy Office

22^d Dec^r 1813
[blank page]

_Private_

My dear Sir

I congratulate you, the -
Navy Board have just come
to a resolution to grant you the
sum of £200 or two hundred
guineas, I know not which for
your care and attention to the
Queen Charlotte & other matters.
I hope you will not think
that you are not paid for
the services rendered I assure
you

you that I wish the sum had
been greater. I am Sir
Always Yours
J Knowles

Navy Office
1 June 1814

[Address panel of a folded letter]

Service
[Black ink stamp: Strand Co]
[Red ink circular stamp: PAID / 1 JU / 1814]

James Sowerby Esq^r
Mead Place
Near the Asylum
Lambeth

J.K.

[Red wax seal with coat of arms]

My dear Sir

If you will have the goodness
to call upon me at this office on
Saturday next being a bill for Two
hundred guineas upon the Treasurer
of the Navy will be ready for you.
Come about 12 o'Clock - If I may
advise I would write a short
letter of thanks to the Navy Board
acknowledging the receipt of their
letter and stating that you were
obliged by their reward as it was
a proof that they thought you
had

had been of service to the Navy,
and that you would direct your
future attention to the subjects I am

Dear Sir Very truly Y^rs
J Knowles

Navy Office
Jan^y. 10^th 1814

[This is the address panel of a folded letter]

[Archival marking in pencil: 8+J.100%]

[Postmark 1: Red circular stamp: PAID / JA 10 / 1814]
[Postmark 2: Black circular stamp: T.P / Piccadilly]

James Sowerby Esq^r
Near the Asylum.
Lambeth

J Knowles

[Red wax seal with an armorial crest]

Dear Sir

M^r Phipps has some
friends who are very
desirous of seeing your
collection of fossils &c &
will it be convenient to
wait upon you tomorrow
or any other day, if so
pray say when I am
Dear Sir
Y^r devoted S^t
J Knowles

Navy Office   }
1^st March 1814

Dear Sir

I was coming to your
Office Tomorrow with your
Son & Mr [unclear: Sospens?] Col^l Jeffersons
Hygrometer and to answer
to the question about perfect
Stone & Thanks. Any time
Tomorrow afterwards or other
Day, if I can be informed
when I shall be likely to
see it. I found it – say
Tomorrow at 12 oclock if you
think it will do

I am Sir
very faithfully
JS

My dear Sir

We have written to
Portsmouth and
directed, that if any
of the charred Wood
taken from His Maj^ys
Ship Dauntless remain
that it is to be sent
in the first Iron, Boat
to Deptford, when it

will

will be forwarded to
You I am, D Sir
Very truly Y^s
J Knowles

Navy Office
11 Mar 1814

---

I saw some of the Charred
Wood at the office afterwards and
some was to have been sent to
me with the Hygrometers but
none was sent – so late as
Aug^t 3. 1814 –

[This is an address panel from a folded letter. The paper is torn at the top edge.]

To
James Sowerby Esq^r
Mead Place

[Red wax seal with a classical-style male bust in profile, facing left.]

My dear Sir

We have recalled all the
Hygrometers. Will you have the
goodneß to give me the addreß of
the man who colours their faces.

I am
My dear Sir
Very truly Y^rs
John Knowles

Navy Office
25 June 1814.

[At the bottom of the page, written upside down:]

My dear Sir
I saw you on the eveng of the 24^th inst
regarding the recall of the Hygrometers. I shall be very much obliged
if you'll let me have an account of their pretty doings up to the time
they have been down &c &c.
I am My dear Sir
yours very sincerely
J K

James Sowerby Esq^r

My dear Sir

If you should be passing this way,
our friend. M^r Seppings will be obliged to

My dear Sir
Very respectfully Yrs
John Knowles

Navy Office
22 Aug^t 1814

My Dear Sir
Jan^y 23. 1815

I take the liberty to trouble you with
the enclosed to forward to the Honourable Navy
Board if you think it [inserted above: correct.] right. I am
Dear Sir
Yours very faithfully
Ja. Sowerby

To Jn^o Knowles Esq^r

---

The Honourable Commissioners of his Majesty's
Navy D^r to
Ja. Sowerby

1814 Oct^r To regulating & Colouring 16 Hygrometers
£8..8..0

1813 Nov^r 27 to 1814 Oct^r 6.
To attending at Deptford seven days
& with Letters & answers regarding Stacking
Timber &c, pursuant to [inserted above: an] express desire of
your Hon. Board expressed 26 Nov^r 1813
} £36..15..0

£45..3..0

Dear Sir
I rec^d your note of the 5^th July. I feel much for
you on account of your loss - it is a sort of trial we must
all undergo and I hope you will bear it with the resignation due
to the giver of Fathers &c I presume I need not trouble M^r
Jones or his old cloaths man about the Hygrometer, especially
as it is so long since. I sent a bill for colouring
the Hygrometers &c on the 23^d Jan^y and not having seen nor
heard since I supposed all was forgotten; perhaps
you will find it, if not I can send you a copy if
you please. I am much engaged in my profession, so
that I am always at home, where I shall be happy
to see you or your friends at any time.
I am Dear Sir,
Yours most truly
Ja^s Sowerby

1815 July 6.
2 Mead Place
Lambeth

To Jn^o Knowles Esq^r

[Address panel on the reverse of the letter. The writing is extremely faint and mostly illegible. The text is oriented upside down relative to the letter's content on the following pages.]

[Postmark: illegible circular stamp]

[unclear: Ja^s?] Sowerby Esq^r
Mead Place
Lambeth

[Postmark: illegible stamp]

My dear Sir

I have received your
last letter, for which
accept my best thanks.
I have made enquiry
but cannot find that
we are minus in the
number of our Hygrometers.
You mention to have
sent to me a Bill for
colouring the Hygrometers.
I really believe never to
have received this, or if
it

it came to hand it has
entirely escaped my memory.
I have lately lost my
father and have consequently
removed to London (N^o. 29.
Gerrard S^t.) where I shall be
happy to see you. I have
intended for some time
past to call upon you
but have been prevented
by untoward circumstances.
I will however make
an early call Yrs truly
J Knowles

Navy Office
5^th July 1815

My Dear Sir,

Lest I should not see you when calling this morn: I trouble you with this to say that if you will look at the enclosed and [inserted above: present] it [inserted above: to them] when you see conveniency you will greatly oblige them Sir, yours faithfully

J. Sy

J Knowles Esq^r

My Dear Sir I am sorry to have kept your instrument so long I now return it with [text crossed out: the scale] [inserted above: some] [text crossed out: observations] [inserted above: remarks] for [text crossed out: your] [inserted above: the] consideration during [text crossed out: your] [inserted above: the] general inspection [text crossed out: of the ship] [inserted above: at Woolwich] [text crossed out: and] [inserted above: which I have] [text crossed out: I have] [text crossed out: drawn] [inserted above: drawn] on vellum to represent by colour

being
The scale being 390
The yellow denotes the dryest part of the wood the safest part
Blue approaching Dangerous to [inserted above: perfect] [text crossed out: illegible] Decay.

I find some of these instruments have been distributed, but as [inserted above: yet] a few [unclear: only?] have been no instructions distributed as I believe there is not, and I should think it advisable to have them all colored

Thankfull for all Friends kindnesses I am Dear Sir,
yours faithfully J Sy

Commissioner Cunningham

My Dear Sir, I thought proper first to have [inserted above: the original and ingenious] brought or sent the [unclear: plan?] from M^r Lukin the hygrometer but after getting it neatly done I thought proper to see how it accorded with the original from whence I made for my report & and finding something of unexpected difference sent it the maker, but he will not [inserted above: be] [text crossed out: again] prevent a [inserted above: an interview or] consultation so soon as I can settle how it is with him, will hast to get it properly done in the mean time I may better to avoid distributing any more of them too late or inconvenient till I have seen him and I am apprehensive they all should be marked as I intend to do it soon as possible, I was at Deptford and saw Com^r Cunningham who says two were sent to Woolwich, and he will as I desired keep them in his charge untill further instructions &c I am Dear Sir yours faithfully J Sy

To J Knowles Esq^r

[Text written vertically on the left margin:]

I had the pleasure of receiving yesterday the [unclear: Curiosities?]. I am highly obliged to you. I have been long to send a few spec: in the desire that they may be in some wise acceptable and of use

Navy Board or M^r Seppings's

Private

Dear Sir

Since I did myself the pleasure of calling upon you with M^r Rackett I have frequently stated both at the Admiralty and this Board the necessity of taking your opinion on the subject of the dry-rot in the Queen Charlotte. I find at length, that they have [inserted above: you to attend] summoned, [text crossed out: them] tomorrow at 3 o'clock if you could call upon me before that hour I shall be glad to have some conversation with you - Any one will shew you my room in this office - M^r Rackett has left London. I am with respect

Y^r obed^t Serv^t

John Knowles

Navy Office

Wednesday-

[Faint text below signature: Surveyors Office / [unclear: S^r Rob^t Seppings at Woolwich?]]
